skill.name: azure-mgmt-fabric-py
skill.description: Guidance for using the Azure Fabric Management SDK for Python to create, inspect, scale, suspend, resume, and delete Microsoft Fabric capacities.
skill.summary: Manage Microsoft Fabric capacities with the Azure Python management SDK.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many Markdown backticks as external command execution and three weak-crypto indicators, but review found no executable Ruby, shell backtick use, or cryptographic code. The skill is publishable with warning because its examples authenticate to Azure and include mutating Fabric capacity operations such as create, update, suspend, resume, and delete.

medium_findings.title: Privileged Azure Resource Mutation Examples
medium_findings.description: The skill provides examples for creating, updating, suspending, resuming, and deleting Microsoft Fabric capacities. These operations are legitimate for this SDK guide, but misuse could change billing state, availability, or resource ownership in an Azure subscription.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced examples directly call Azure management SDK methods that mutate or delete Fabric capacity resources. This is expected for the skill domain, but the operational impact is clear.

low_findings.title: Environment Variable Access for Azure Configuration
low_findings.description: The examples read AZURE_SUBSCRIPTION_ID and AZURE_RESOURCE_GROUP from environment variables. These values are configuration identifiers rather than secrets, and no exfiltration path is present in the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The environment access is visible in simple SDK examples and is limited to Azure resource selection. I found no evidence that the values are logged, transmitted to third-party services, or used for command construction.

low_findings.title: Static Backtick Command Alerts Are Markdown False Positives
low_findings.description: The external command alerts are caused by Markdown code fences and inline code formatting. The only shell commands shown are package installation commands for azure-mgmt-fabric and azure-identity.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review shows fenced Bash and Python examples plus Markdown tables, not Ruby backtick execution. The shell content is limited to standard pip installation commands.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The scanner flagged weak cryptography at the skill description and Fabric SKU or state tables. Those lines describe Microsoft Fabric capacity names and states, and contain no cryptographic algorithm selection or hashing logic.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain YAML description text and Markdown tables for Fabric SKUs and capacity states. I found no crypto APIs, hashes, cipher names, or security-sensitive transformations in those locations.

dangerous_patterns.title: Azure Management Calls With Side Effects
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill demonstrates Azure SDK calls that alter live Microsoft Fabric capacity resources. Users should run them only against intended subscriptions and resource groups.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The listed SDK calls perform create, update, and delete operations. The pattern is legitimate administration code, but it has real cloud-resource impact.

content.user_title: Manage Microsoft Fabric Capacities in Python
content.value_statement: Managing Microsoft Fabric capacity through Azure APIs can be error-prone and hard to start. This skill gives focused Python SDK examples for authentication, capacity lifecycle operations, SKU checks, and long-running operations.
actual_capabilities: Shows how to install azure-mgmt-fabric and azure-identity packages.
Demonstrates DefaultAzureCredential setup with a FabricMgmtClient.
Provides examples for creating, reading, listing, updating, suspending, resuming, and deleting Fabric capacities.
Documents capacity name availability checks and available SKU listing.
Explains common Fabric SKUs, capacity states, and long-running operation handling.
limitations: Does not validate permissions, subscription policy, or Azure tenant configuration.
Does not include automated tests, reusable modules, or production error handling patterns.
Uses placeholder values that users must replace with their own resource names and administrator identities.
Does not cover monitoring implementation beyond a brief best-practice mention.

use_cases.title: Automate Fabric Capacity Provisioning
use_cases.description: Create and configure Fabric capacities from Python scripts using Azure identity and management clients.

use_cases.title: Inspect Subscription Capacity Inventory
use_cases.description: List Fabric capacities by resource group or subscription and review names, SKUs, states, and regions.

use_cases.title: Control Capacity Cost States
use_cases.description: Suspend unused capacities and resume them when workloads need compute capacity again.

prompt_templates.title: Set Up the Client
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for authentication and client creation.
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me how to use this skill to install the required packages and create a FabricMgmtClient with DefaultAzureCredential.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Capacity
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me adapt the capacity creation example for my Azure resource group, region, SKU, and capacity administrator list.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Current Capacities
prompt_templates.scenario: Operational review across a resource group or subscription.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to outline a Python workflow that lists Fabric capacities and reports their name, SKU, state, and location.

prompt_templates.title: Build a Lifecycle Automation Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a safe Python automation plan for checking name availability, creating a capacity, scaling it, suspending it, and handling long-running operations.

output_examples.input: A user asks how to start managing Fabric capacities with Python.
output_examples.output: A concise setup path covering package installation, Azure authentication, required environment variables, and FabricMgmtClient creation.

output_examples.input: A platform engineer wants to scale a Fabric capacity.
output_examples.output: A step-by-step explanation of the update workflow, the SKU fields to change, and the need to wait for the long-running operation to finish.

output_examples.input: An administrator needs to reduce cost for an unused capacity.
output_examples.output: Guidance to verify the target resource group and capacity name, suspend the capacity, and confirm the resulting state before assuming billing changed.

best_practices: Confirm the subscription ID, resource group, and capacity name before running mutating examples.
Use least-privilege Azure identities and avoid placing credentials directly in prompts or files.
Wait for long-running operations to complete and check final capacity state before continuing.
anti_patterns: Running delete, suspend, or scale operations against production resources without approval.
Hardcoding tenant credentials, user identities, or subscription values into shared examples.
Assuming create or update calls finish immediately without polling the operation result.

faq.question: What Azure service does this skill cover?
faq.answer: It covers Microsoft Fabric capacity management through the Azure Fabric Management SDK for Python.

faq.question: Does it create production-ready automation?
faq.answer: No. It provides focused SDK examples that users must adapt with validation, error handling, and approval controls.

faq.question: Which authentication method is shown?
faq.answer: The examples use DefaultAzureCredential from azure-identity.

faq.question: Can it change live Azure resources?
faq.answer: Yes. The examples include create, update, suspend, resume, and delete operations for Fabric capacities.

faq.question: Does it require environment variables?
faq.answer: The examples read subscription and resource group values from environment variables.

faq.question: Does it explain Fabric SKU sizes?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes a table of Fabric SKU names and their capacity unit counts.
